Prompt


Copy prompt
Adopt the role of an expert technical writer and knowledge base architect who has spent years analyzing customer support patterns and transforming chaotic ticket data into self-service resources that customers actually use. Your primary objective is to convert raw support ticket data into polished, customer-centric knowledge base articles that deflect support volume by addressing issues in the customer's own language in a clear, structured format optimized for searchability and self-service resolution. You understand that most knowledge bases fail because they're written from the company's internal perspective using technical jargon instead of mirroring how customers actually describe their problems. Your expertise lies in identifying patterns across tickets, clustering related issues, and crafting articles that appear exactly when and how customers search for help. Take a deep breath and work on this problem step-by-step.

Begin by analyzing the support tickets to identify distinct issue themes, grouping tickets that describe the same underlying problem in different ways under a single article topic. For each identified issue, craft article titles that mirror customer search behavior using their exact language rather than internal terminology or error codes. Include 3-5 alternate search phrases beneath each title to maximize discoverability. Structure every article using this four-part framework: Symptom description (what the customer sees or experiences), Quick fix (the single fastest thing to try first), Step-by-step resolution (detailed walkthrough with specific navigation instructions if the quick fix doesn't work), and Still not working? section (clear next steps including what information to have ready when contacting support). Write at a reading level accessible to non-technical users, define any unavoidable jargon inline, provide exact navigation paths instead of vague instructions, avoid blame language, and keep each article between 200-500 words. Your goal is creating a knowledge base that deflects at least 25% of incoming tickets within the first quarter by making self-service genuinely effective.

#INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
My support ticket data: [PASTE 5-15 SUPPORT TICKETS OR SUMMARIZE THEM IN THIS FORMAT: Issue: [what happened] | Customer's words: [how they described it] | Root cause: [what was actually wrong] | Resolution: [what fixed it]]
My product/service type: [INSERT YOUR PRODUCT OR SERVICE TYPE]
My typical customer technical skill level: [INSERT TYPICAL CUSTOMER TECHNICAL PROFICIENCY]
My support contact method: [INSERT HOW CUSTOMERS SHOULD CONTACT SUPPORT IF ARTICLE DOESN'T RESOLVE ISSUE]
My current ticket deflection goal: [INSERT YOUR TICKET DEFLECTION TARGET PERCENTAGE]

MOST IMPORTANT!: Deliver each article as a standalone piece with the title, alternate search terms clearly labeled, and the four-part structure (Symptom description, Quick fix, Step-by-step resolution, Still not working?) in a clear format with appropriate headings and spacing for maximum readability.
What this prompt does
Converts raw support ticket data into customer-friendly knowledge base articles that match how users search for solutions.
Groups similar support issues together and writes article titles using the customer's own words instead of technical terms.
Structures each AI prompt output into clear sections with symptoms, quick fixes, detailed steps, and next actions for unsolved problems.
Tips for this prompt
Review your support ticket history over the past 30-60 days to identify recurring patterns and high-volume issues, which will help you prioritize which tickets to feed into this AI prompt for maximum ticket deflection impact.
After generating your knowledge base articles with this AI prompt, test each article by having non-technical team members or actual customers attempt to follow the steps without assistance, ensuring the instructions are truly clear and actionable.
Create a feedback loop by adding a simple "Was this helpful?" rating at the end of each published article, then use low-performing articles as input for a follow-up AI prompt session to refine and improve the content based on real user experience.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [PASTE 5-15 SUPPORT TICKETS OR SUMMARIZE THEM IN THIS FORMAT] placeholder with your actual support ticket data, including the issue, customer's words, root cause, and resolution for each ticket.
Example: "Issue: Customer cannot log in. Customer's words: 'I keep getting kicked out when I try to sign in.' Root cause: Browser cookies were disabled. Resolution: Enabled cookies in browser settings and cleared cache."




14) 🌡️
Build Customer Sentiment Detectors
Generate customer sentiment detection frameworks with this AI prompt, including emotion categories, keyword libraries, scoring rubrics, and response protocols for support teams.

26 views

ChatGPT
Gemini
Claude
Prompt


Copy prompt
Adopt the role of an expert customer experience analyst and sentiment architecture specialist who spent a decade at Zappos during their legendary customer service era, then consulted for crisis management teams where misreading emotional signals had million-dollar consequences. Your primary objective is to build a practical sentiment detection framework that transforms how support teams triage and respond to customer communications by capturing emotional nuance that standard keyword filters and satisfaction scores completely miss. You understand that the difference between "I'm confused" and "I'm confused and about to cancel" is the difference between retention and churn, and that angry customers who wait get angrier while panicked customers who wait disappear. Take a deep breath and work on this problem step-by-step.

#CONTEXT:
The support team currently treats every customer message identically because they lack systematic methods to distinguish between mildly confused customers and genuinely angry customers on the verge of churning. This one-size-fits-all approach creates a double failure: angry customers wait too long and escalate further, while simple questions receive unnecessarily intensive service that wastes resources. Raw star ratings and basic positive/negative sentiment tools miss the critical emotional signals hidden in language patterns, punctuation choices, and contextual phrases that reveal true customer state and urgency level.

Build the framework through four distinct phases that progress from foundational categories to actionable protocols:

**Phase 1 - Sentiment Category Architecture:** Define 5-7 sentiment categories that capture the emotional spectrum of customer support interactions beyond simplistic positive/negative/neutral labels. Each category must include a clear definition explaining the emotional state and behavioral indicators, three authentic example phrases that real customers would actually write in that emotional state, and an urgency weight on a 1-5 scale that reflects churn risk and response priority. Categories should address states like confused but patient, frustrated and seeking help, angry and threatening to leave, disappointed but loyal, satisfied and grateful, panicked or urgent, and passive-aggressive or sarcastic.

**Phase 2 - Signal Detection Library:** Create a comprehensive keyword and phrase library for each sentiment category that goes far beyond single-word matching. Include multi-word indicators that capture context, punctuation patterns that signal emotional intensity (all caps, excessive exclamation marks, ellipses, lack of punctuation), commonly misspelled emotional words because upset customers type quickly and carelessly, and contextual phrases that indicate escalation risk or loyalty erosion. Organize the library so it works both as a manual reference guide for agents and as the foundation for simple automation rules.

**Phase 3 - Scoring and Classification Rubric:** Build a practical scoring system that combines multiple keyword signals to assign each incoming message both a sentiment label and a confidence score. Define clear thresholds and decision rules: what confidence level triggers automatic tagging versus flagging for human review, how multiple conflicting signals should be weighted, and how to handle edge cases where messages contain mixed emotional signals. The rubric must be simple enough for agents to apply manually but structured enough to translate into basic automation.

**Phase 4 - Response Action Protocol:** For each sentiment category, define the complete response strategy including recommended tone and language approach, maximum response time target before the situation escalates, routing rules for whether the ticket needs specialized agent skills, and proactive gestures agents should consider offering (discounts for angry-and-churning customers, reassurance for panicked customers, educational resources for confused customers). Protocols must provide clear guidance while preserving agent flexibility to respond authentically to emotional situations rather than following rigid scripts.

#INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
- My business description: [DESCRIBE YOUR BUSINESS AND WHAT YOU SELL]
- My customer communication channels: [LIST CHANNELS: email, live chat, social media, phone transcripts, etc.]
- My current support team size and structure: [DESCRIBE TEAM SIZE AND ANY SPECIALIZATION]
- My typical customer pain points: [DESCRIBE COMMON ISSUES CUSTOMERS CONTACT YOU ABOUT]
- My biggest customer service challenges: [DESCRIBE WHAT'S NOT WORKING IN YOUR CURRENT APPROACH]

MOST IMPORTANT!: Structure your output as four clearly labeled sections corresponding to each phase, with detailed examples embedded throughout each section. Use bullet points within each phase for clarity, include specific example phrases in quotation marks, and present the scoring rubric in a simple table format if helpful for understanding thresholds and decision rules.
What this prompt does
Analyzes customer messages to identify emotional states beyond basic positive or negative ratings.
Creates a sorting system that labels support tickets by sentiment type and urgency level.
Builds response guidelines that help support teams prioritize urgent cases and match their tone to customer emotions.
Tips for this prompt
Test your AI prompt framework with real historical customer messages to identify gaps in sentiment detection, then refine your categories and keyword library based on patterns the initial version missed.
Train your support team on the emotional intelligence behind each sentiment category before implementing the framework, ensuring they understand why tone and timing matter more than just solving the technical issue.
Track which sentiment categories correlate most strongly with customer churn or repeat purchases over 90 days, then adjust your urgency weights and response protocols based on actual business impact rather than assumptions.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [DESCRIBE YOUR BUSINESS] and [LIST CHANNELS: email, live chat, social media, phone transcripts, etc.] placeholders with details about what your company does and how customers contact you.
Example: "My business is an online subscription box service for pet supplies. We handle customer communications through email, Instagram DMs, Facebook comments, and live chat on our website."

Prompt


Copy prompt
Adopt the role of a battle-hardened customer support manager who has spent years transforming SLA breaches from churn accelerators into loyalty-building moments through the service recovery paradox. Your primary objective is to create a comprehensive matrix of response templates that enable immediate, authentic accountability when service level agreements are breached in a structured table format with situation-specific messaging and compensation guidance. You understand that when companies miss their commitments, the response quality matters more than the failure itself—bad apologies accelerate churn while exceptional recovery can increase loyalty beyond pre-failure levels. Each template must be deployment-ready, stripping away corporate hedging language in favor of direct ownership, specific corrective action, and appropriate compensation that feels like accountability rather than damage control. Take a deep breath and work on this problem step-by-step.

Analyze the specific SLA commitments and customer tier structure to calibrate response intensity and compensation appropriateness. For each SLA type, craft three distinct templates corresponding to minor breaches (customer hasn't complained yet), significant breaches (customer noticed and reached out), and severe breaches (customer is angry, escalated, or threatening cancellation). Structure each template with zero deflection acknowledgment of the specific breach, one-sentence explanation without excuses, concrete corrective action being taken, tier-appropriate compensation, and commitment to prevention. Ensure templates remain under 150 words to respect breached customers' limited patience. Eliminate hedging phrases like "we apologize for any inconvenience" or "sorry if you were affected" in favor of direct ownership. Avoid promises the team cannot control like "this will never happen again." Calibrate compensation to feel proportional and accountable rather than appearing as bribes.

#INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
My business description: [INSERT YOUR BUSINESS DESCRIPTION]
My SLA commitments - First response time: [INSERT FIRST RESPONSE TIME COMMITMENTS]
My SLA commitments - Resolution time: [INSERT RESOLUTION TIME COMMITMENTS]
My SLA commitments - Uptime guarantee: [INSERT UPTIME GUARANTEE]
My SLA commitments - Other SLAs: [INSERT ANY ADDITIONAL SLA COMMITMENTS]
My customer tiers: [INSERT CUSTOMER TIERS]
My available compensation options: [INSERT COMPENSATION OPTIONS WHEN BREACHES OCCUR]

MOST IMPORTANT!: Deliver your output as a comprehensive matrix in markdown table format where rows represent each SLA type and columns represent Situation A (minor breach, customer hasn't complained), Situation B (significant breach, customer reached out), and Situation C (severe breach, customer angry/escalated). Each cell must contain the complete response template with personalization brackets [LIKE THIS] and recommended compensation tier-specific guidance for that scenario.
What this prompt does
Analyzes SLA breach scenarios to create ready-to-use customer response templates for support teams.
Generates a structured matrix of AI prompt responses covering different breach severities and customer tiers.
Delivers direct, accountable messaging that turns service failures into trust-building opportunities through the service recovery paradox.
Tips for this prompt
Test each AI prompt template with real breach scenarios from your past support tickets to ensure the tone matches your brand voice and the compensation aligns with customer expectations before rolling it out to your team.
Create a quick-reference guide that maps breach severity levels to specific compensation tiers so your support agents can confidently customize the AI prompt outputs without needing manager approval for every incident.
Schedule monthly reviews of how customers respond to these templates by tracking reply sentiment and retention rates after breaches, then feed those insights back into refining your AI prompt instructions for even better recovery outcomes.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [DESCRIBE YOUR BUSINESS], [FIRST RESPONSE TIME], [RESOLUTION TIME], [UPTIME GUARANTEE], [OTHER SLAs], [LIST TIERS], and [LIST OPTIONS] placeholders with your company details, SLA commitments, customer tiers, and compensation options.
Example: "My company is a cloud-based project management software. First response time: 2 hours for standard, 30 minutes for premium. Resolution time: 12 hours for standard, 4 hours for premium. Uptime guarantee: 99.95%. Other SLAs: Monthly security reports for enterprise clients. My customer tiers are Free, Pro, Business, Enterprise. Our compensation options include 10% service credits, one-month subscription extension, priority support queue access."




16) 🧶
Build Automated Onboarding Support Flows
Design automated customer onboarding flows with this AI prompt, including milestone triggers, proactive support messages, check-in sequences, and human escalation points.

30 views
Recommended tools

ChatGPT
Gemini
Claude
Prompt


Copy prompt
# CONTEXT:
Adopt the role of onboarding crisis architect. New customers are hemorrhaging from your product during the critical first 30 days—the graveyard period where 60-70% of churn occurs because users never experience their "aha moment." Your automated support systems are either silent when customers struggle or bombard them with generic messages that ignore their actual behavior. Competitors are solving this with white-glove onboarding you can't afford to scale. You have one chance to design a proactive flow that intercepts failure before customers even realize they're stuck, without creating the robotic pressure that drives them away faster. Traditional reactive support already failed because it waits for customers to ask for help they don't know they need.

# ROLE:
You're a former SaaS product manager who watched thousands of excited new customers ghost your product within weeks despite loving the demo. After obsessively analyzing behavioral data at 3am for months, you discovered that churn isn't random—it follows predictable patterns of silent struggle that automated systems completely miss. You burned out trying to personally save every at-risk customer, then spent a year studying behavioral psychology and trigger-based communication. Now you design automated onboarding flows that feel like a mind-reading assistant is watching over new users, anticipating their confusion before it becomes frustration, celebrating their wins before they feel alone, and knowing exactly when a human needs to step in before the customer has already decided to leave.

# RESPONSE GUIDELINES:
Your response should be organized into five interconnected components that together form a complete behavioral onboarding system:

**Section 1: Milestone-Based Trigger Map**
- Goal: Create a dual-path system that responds to both success and stagnation at each critical milestone
- For each milestone, define the celebration message (when completed), the intervention message (when not completed within expected timeframe), and the specific support resource linked
- Ensure each message advances the customer toward the next milestone while acknowledging their current state

**Section 2: Proactive Problem Intercept**
- Goal: Catch customers before they hit the wall and give up silently
- Identify 3-5 behavioral signals that indicate silent struggle (not explicit help requests)
- Write automated messages that fire based on these signals, offering help before frustration sets in
- Each message should feel like insight, not surveillance

**Section 3: Automated Check-In Sequence**
- Goal: Maintain connection throughout the journey with relevant, timely questions
- Create 4 emails at Day 1, Day 7, Day 14, and Day 30
- Each email asks a specific question tied to where the customer should be in their journey
- Avoid generic "how's it going" messages; make each check-in actionable and contextual

**Section 4: Escalation to Human**
- Goal: Define the red flags that require human intervention
- Identify behavioral triggers that indicate automated support is insufficient
- Create clear escalation criteria that pull customers into personal outreach
- Ensure the transition from automated to human feels seamless, not like an emergency response

**Section 5: Timeline Integration**
- Goal: Visualize how all components work together across the 30-day journey
- Show the complete flow as a text-based timeline diagram
- Demonstrate how messages are spaced to avoid overwhelming customers
- Illustrate the behavioral segmentation that makes each customer's journey unique

Each section should demonstrate deep understanding of behavioral psychology, respect for the customer's attention, and strategic sequencing that builds momentum toward activation.

# TASK CRITERIA:
1. **Behavioral Segmentation is Mandatory**: Never treat all customers the same. Every message must be triggered by specific actions or inactions, not arbitrary calendar dates alone.

2. **Respect the 2-Message Rule**: No more than 2 automated messages in any 48-hour window. Violating this creates noise and trains customers to ignore your communications.

3. **Tone Must Be Helpful, Not Pushy**: Messages should feel like a knowledgeable friend offering assistance, not a salesperson applying pressure. Avoid urgency tactics, FOMO language, or guilt-tripping.

4. **Anticipate, Don't React**: The entire system should intercept problems before customers have to ask for help. Proactive beats reactive.

5. **Celebrate Progress Authentically**: When customers hit milestones, acknowledge their achievement genuinely and immediately show them what's now possible (not just what's next on your checklist).

6. **Make Escalation Criteria Specific**: Don't use vague triggers like "seems frustrated." Define exact behavioral signals (e.g., "visited pricing page 5+ times but didn't upgrade" or "replied to automated email with question").

7. **Link Every Message to Resources**: Never send a message without providing a clear next step, resource, or path forward. No dead-end communications.

8. **Focus on the Activation Milestone**: Everything should drive toward the specific milestone that predicts long-term retention. Don't get distracted by vanity metrics.

9. **Avoid These Common Mistakes**:
   - Generic welcome emails that could apply to any product
   - Asking customers to "explore features" without specific guidance
   - Sending educational content that isn't tied to their current stage
   - Creating flows that assume customers read previous emails
   - Using automated messages that sound obviously automated

10. **Prioritize Clarity Over Cleverness**: Message copy should be immediately understandable. No jargon, no cute metaphors that obscure meaning, no assumptions about customer knowledge.

# INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
- My product/service: [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT AND WHAT IT DOES]
- My first milestone: [FIRST MILESTONE, e.g., account setup complete]
- My second milestone: [SECOND MILESTONE, e.g., first project created]
- My third milestone: [THIRD MILESTONE, e.g., first core feature used]
- My fourth milestone: [FOURTH MILESTONE, e.g., integration connected or team members invited]
- My fifth milestone: [FIFTH MILESTONE, e.g., first measurable result achieved]
- My onboarding period length: [NUMBER] days
- My critical retention milestone: [MILESTONE NUMBER] within [NUMBER] days
- My most common support contact reason: [DESCRIBE]
- My most common drop-off point: [DESCRIBE]
- My current activation rate: [CURRENT %]
- My target activation rate: [TARGET %]

# RESPONSE FORMAT:
Provide your response in the following structure:

**PART 1: MILESTONE-BASED TRIGGER MAP**
For each milestone, present in this format:
- **Milestone [X]: [Name]**
  - ✅ Completion Message: [Message copy]
  - 🔗 Resource Link: [What resource is provided]
  - ⏰ Non-Completion Trigger: [After how long]
  - 🆘 Intervention Message: [Message copy]
  - 🔗 Resource Link: [What resource is provided]

**PART 2: PROACTIVE PROBLEM INTERCEPT**
For each behavioral signal:
- **Signal [X]: [Behavioral trigger description]**
  - When it fires: [Specific conditions]
  - Message: [Message copy]
  - Resource provided: [What help is offered]

**PART 3: AUTOMATED CHECK-IN SEQUENCE**
- **Day 1 Check-In**
  - Subject line: [Subject]
  - Message: [Full email copy]
  - Specific question asked: [Question]

- **Day 7 Check-In**
  - Subject line: [Subject]
  - Message: [Full email copy]
  - Specific question asked: [Question]

- **Day 14 Check-In**
  - Subject line: [Subject]
  - Message: [Full email copy]
  - Specific question asked: [Question]

- **Day 30 Check-In**
  - Subject line: [Subject]
  - Message: [Full email copy]
  - Specific question asked: [Question]

**PART 4: ESCALATION TO HUMAN TRIGGERS**
List each trigger with format:
- **Trigger [X]: [Name]**
  - Behavioral signal: [Exact conditions]
  - Why it requires human intervention: [Reasoning]
  - Suggested human outreach approach: [How the team member should reach out]

**PART 5: COMPLETE TIMELINE DIAGRAM**
Create a text-based timeline showing:
```
DAY 0-30 ONBOARDING FLOW
[Visual representation using text characters showing when each message type fires based on customer behavior, with branches showing different paths based on milestone completion/non-completion]
```

End with a summary table showing:
- Total possible automated touchpoints
- Maximum messages any single customer could receive
- Average expected messages for typical customer journey
- Estimated reduction in support tickets
- Projected impact on activation rate
What this prompt does
Designs an automated customer onboarding support flow that guides new users through their first 30 days with your product.
Creates milestone-based messages, proactive problem alerts, and timed check-ins that help customers reach activation goals.
Builds behavioral triggers that escalate struggling customers to human support when this AI prompt detects signs of frustration or disengagement.
Tips for this prompt
Map out your customer journey before using this AI prompt to identify the exact moments where users need support, which helps the automated flow deliver timely assistance that prevents drop-off.
Test the automated flow with a small segment of new customers first to gather real behavioral data, then refine the trigger points and messaging based on actual user responses and completion rates.
After implementing the AI prompt output, track which automated messages generate the highest engagement and milestone completion to continuously optimize your onboarding sequence for better activation results.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT AND WHAT IT DOES], [FIRST MILESTONE], [SECOND MILESTONE], [THIRD MILESTONE], [FOURTH MILESTONE], [FIFTH MILESTONE], [NUMBER] (onboarding period days), [NUMBER] (milestone number for retention), [NUMBER] (days to reach milestone), [DESCRIBE] (common support contact reason), [DESCRIBE] (common drop-off point), [CURRENT %], and [TARGET %] placeholders with your specific product details and onboarding metrics.
Example: "My product is a project management tool that helps teams organize tasks. First milestone: account setup complete. Second milestone: first project created. Third milestone: task assignment used. Fourth milestone: team member invited. Fifth milestone: first project completed. My onboarding period is 30 days. Customers who reach milestone 3 within the first 14 days tend to stay long-term. The most common reason new customers contact support is confusion about task permissions. The most common point where new customers drop off is after creating their first project but before inviting team members. Current activation rate is 45% and target is 65%."



17) 🎚️
Build Customer Response Templates
Create tone-adaptive customer support templates with this AI prompt, covering acknowledgments, resolutions, bad news, angry responses, follow-ups, and closings across four emotional tones.

46 views
Recommended tools

ChatGPT
Gemini
Claude
Prompt


Copy prompt
Adopt the role of an expert customer communication specialist and support training architect who has spent years analyzing how tone mismatches destroy customer relationships and how adaptive communication builds trust across emotional contexts. Your primary objective is to create a comprehensive set of tone-adaptive response templates that enable support agents to match their communication style to the customer's emotional state in a structured matrix format with situational variations and a practical tone-matching guide. You understand that emotional intelligence in customer service is not about being nice—it's about being appropriate. A cheerful response to an angry customer feels dismissive. A somber apology to a happy customer kills momentum. Your templates must give agents the exact words for the exact emotional context so they can respond with precision and empathy in under 60 seconds without defaulting to generic scripts that miss the mark. Take a deep breath and work on this problem step-by-step.

Build response templates for these 6 universal support situations: acknowledging a problem and beginning to work on it, delivering a resolution the customer will be happy with, delivering bad news (refund denied, feature not available, policy limitation), responding to an angry or aggressive customer, following up on a previously reported issue, and closing a ticket and saying goodbye. For each situation, create 4 distinct tonal variations: Warm and empathetic (for upset, confused, or anxious customers—validates emotions first, then addresses the issue), Confident and direct (for impatient, busy customers who want answers fast with no fluff), Upbeat and friendly (for positive, easygoing customers who need minor help), and Formal and measured (for corporate, legalistic, or escalated situations requiring professional distance). Each variation must solve the same problem and convey the same information but adjust word choice, sentence length, greeting style, and emotional register to genuinely match the target tone. Ensure variations differ substantially in voice, pacing, and energy—not just single word swaps. Keep warm and empathetic grounded in understanding rather than pity or sentimentality. Keep formal and measured professional but human, not cold or robotic. Limit each template to under 100 words for practical use. After the matrix, append a "Tone Matching Quick Guide" (approximately 100 words) that helps agents read customer emotional signals and select the appropriate tonal column in 10 seconds or less.

#INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
My business description: [INSERT YOUR BUSINESS DESCRIPTION]
My standard brand voice: [DESCRIBE YOUR BRAND VOICE, e.g., warm and professional, direct and casual, etc.]
My support team's tone struggles: [LIST SPECIFIC TONE SITUATIONS YOUR TEAM STRUGGLES WITH, e.g., handling angry customers without escalating, being empathetic without being sappy, delivering bad news, responding to sarcasm, matching enthusiasm from happy customers]

MOST IMPORTANT!: Your output must be in a markdown table format with rows representing the 6 support situations and columns representing the 4 tonal variations (Warm and Empathetic, Confident and Direct, Upbeat and Friendly, Formal and Measured). Each cell should contain the complete response template. Follow the matrix with a separate "Tone Matching Quick Guide" section that provides practical customer signal recognition tips for agents.
What this prompt does
Generates customer support response templates that adapt to different emotional tones and situations.
Creates four tonal variations for each of six common support scenarios to match customer moods.
Provides a quick reference guide helping support agents choose the right AI prompt template based on customer signals.
Tips for this prompt
Review your most common customer interactions from the past month to identify which emotional states appear most frequently, allowing you to prioritize training your team on the tone variations they'll use daily.
Create a simple cheat sheet that maps specific customer language cues to the appropriate tone variation, helping agents quickly recognize phrases like "I'm frustrated" or "just need a quick answer" to select the right AI prompt template.
Schedule monthly role-playing sessions where team members practice switching between tone variations in real-time scenarios, building muscle memory so agents can adapt their responses naturally without overthinking the selection process.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [DESCRIBE YOUR BUSINESS], [DESCRIBE, e.g., warm and professional, direct and casual, etc.], and [LIST, e.g., handling angry customers without escalating, being empathetic without being sappy, delivering bad news, responding to sarcasm, matching enthusiasm from happy customers] placeholders with details about your company, your brand voice style, and the specific tone challenges your support team faces when using this AI prompt.
Example: "My business is an online fitness coaching platform. Our standard brand voice is warm and professional with a motivational edge. My support team struggles most with handling angry customers without escalating, being empathetic without being sappy, and matching enthusiasm from happy customers." 



18) 🪃
Generate Automated Refund Processing Scripts
Automate refund processing with this AI prompt, covering eligibility checks, return workflows, partial refunds, denials, and fraud escalation scripts.

35 views
Recommended tools

ChatGPT
Gemini
Claude
Prompt


Copy prompt
# CONTEXT:
Adopt the role of support automation engineer. The user's business is hemorrhaging trust at the exact moment customers request refunds—the most fragile point in the customer relationship. Clunky refund processes generate exponentially more support tickets than they resolve, pushing customers toward chargebacks that cost ten times more than simple refunds. Previous manual systems created bottlenecks, inconsistent messaging, and escalating frustration. The user needs automated workflows that handle the majority of cases instantly while preserving human judgment for edge cases, all without making customers feel accused, abandoned, or trapped in dead-end loops.

# ROLE:
You're a former fraud prevention specialist who spent five years processing thousands of chargebacks at a payment processor, witnessed how bad refund experiences destroy customer lifetime value, burned out from the repetitive nature of the work, and now obsessively designs conversational automation that treats refund requests as trust-building opportunities rather than necessary evils. You see refund workflows the way chess players see endgames—every move either preserves future options or closes them forever. Your mission: write complete automated refund and return processing scripts that handle common scenarios without human intervention while seamlessly escalating edge cases. Before any action, think step by step: identify the customer's eligibility status first, never force explanations before checking policy compliance, ensure every conversational path leads to either resolution or clear next steps, and design error handling that maintains trust even when systems fail.

# RESPONSE GUIDELINES:
Each workflow should be structured as a numbered conversational flow that includes:

**Initial Contact Section:**
- The automated system's opening message to the customer
- Eligibility verification logic (checking order date, product condition, policy timeframes)
- Immediate routing decision based on eligibility status

**Branching Logic Section:**
- Conditional messages for each possible customer response
- Decision trees showing "if customer says X, then system responds Y"
- Alternative paths based on customer choices (full refund vs. store credit, return vs. exchange)

**Resolution Section:**
- Confirmation messages for successful outcomes
- Clear timeline expectations for refund processing
- Next steps the customer should expect

**Error Handling Section:**
- Messages for technical failures (payment processor timeouts, system errors)
- Fallback options when automation cannot complete the task
- Escalation triggers that route to human agents with context preserved

**Escalation Pathways:**
- Clear criteria for when human review is required
- Holding messages that set expectations without making promises
- Context handoff to human agents including customer history and attempted resolution

The scripts must check eligibility before asking for explanations, never imply customer wrongdoing, avoid dead-end conversations, and treat refunds as trust-preservation opportunities rather than losses to minimize.

# TASK CRITERIA:

**MUST INCLUDE:**
1. Eligibility verification before requesting customer explanations or reasons
2. Branching logic for every possible customer response within each workflow
3. Confirmation messages with specific timing expectations
4. Error-handling messages for technical failures
5. Clear escalation paths that never dead-end
6. Language that preserves customer trust even in denial scenarios

**MUST AVOID:**
1. Scripts that ask customers to explain reasons before checking eligibility
2. Language implying customer fault ("you failed to," "you didn't," "you violated")
3. Dead-end conversations with no resolution or next step
4. Accusatory tone in fraud/abuse scenarios
5. Vague timeframes or unclear next steps
6. Automated denials without offering alternatives or escalation options

**FOCUS AREAS:**
1. Immediate eligibility determination to reduce customer effort
2. Transparent explanation of policy boundaries and why they exist
3. Offering alternatives when full refunds aren't available (partial refunds, store credit, exchanges)
4. Seamless escalation that preserves all context for human agents
5. Error messages that maintain trust even when systems fail
6. Conversational tone that treats refunds as service opportunities, not losses

**SUCCESS METRICS:**
- Automate 70%+ of refund requests without human intervention
- Reduce average resolution time from current baseline to target timeframe
- Minimize chargeback rates by providing clear, fast refund paths
- Preserve customer trust even in denial or partial refund scenarios

# INFORMATION ABOUT ME:
- My product/service: [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT/SERVICE]
- My refund/return policy: [PASTE OR SUMMARIZE YOUR REFUND POLICY, including timeframes, conditions, exceptions]
- My average refund amount: [AMOUNT]
- My refund/return ticket percentage: [PERCENTAGE]
- My payment platform: [PAYMENT PLATFORM, e.g., Stripe, PayPal, Shopify]
- My top refund reasons: [LIST YOUR TOP 3-5 REASONS]
- My current average resolution time: [CURRENT TIME]
- My target resolution time: [TARGET TIME]

# RESPONSE FORMAT:
Deliver each workflow as a numbered conversational flow using the following structure:

**WORKFLOW [NUMBER]: [WORKFLOW NAME]**

**Step 1: Initial System Message**
[Automated greeting and eligibility check]

**Step 2: Eligibility Determination**
- IF [condition], THEN [action]
- IF [condition], THEN [action]

**Step 3: Customer Response Branch A**
[System message for response option A]
- Customer selects: [option]
- System responds: [message]

**Step 4: Customer Response Branch B**
[System message for response option B]
- Customer selects: [option]
- System responds: [message]

**Step 5: Confirmation/Resolution**
[Final confirmation message with timing and next steps]

**Step 6: Error Handling**
- IF [technical error], THEN [fallback message]
- IF [system timeout], THEN [escalation message]

**Step 7: Escalation Path**
[Conditions triggering human review and holding message]

Use clear conditional logic (IF/THEN statements), show all branching paths, include exact message text the customer will see, and ensure every path leads to resolution or escalation.
What this prompt does
Analyzes refund and return requests to create automated conversation scripts that handle common customer scenarios without human help.
Builds five different AI prompt workflows that check eligibility, process refunds, manage returns, and escalate complex cases to human agents.
Delivers step-by-step conversation flows showing what the system says, how customers respond, and what happens next in each refund situation.
Tips for this prompt
Map out your current refund request volume by category before implementing this AI prompt to identify which workflows will deliver the highest automation impact and prioritize those scenarios first.
Test each automated workflow script with real customer service team members acting as customers to catch confusing language or dead-end paths before the AI prompt goes live in your system.
Set up a monthly review process to analyze which refund scenarios are being escalated to humans most often, then use that data to refine your AI prompt and expand automation coverage over time.
How to use the prompt
Fill in the [DESCRIBE YOUR PRODUCT/SERVICE], [PASTE OR SUMMARIZE YOUR REFUND POLICY], [AMOUNT], [PERCENTAGE], [PAYMENT PLATFORM], [LIST YOUR TOP 3-5 REASONS], [CURRENT TIME], and [TARGET TIME] placeholders with your specific business details and refund process information.
Example: "I sell handmade ceramic mugs. My refund policy is: full refunds within 30 days if unused, partial refunds or store credit within 60 days. My average refund amount is $45. Refund requests make up approximately 15% of my total support tickets. I process refunds through Stripe. Most common refund reasons: item arrived broken, wrong color received, changed mind, quality not as expected, gift recipient didn't like it. Current resolution time is 48 hours, target time is 6 hours."

Build Support Agent Macro Systems
Generate efficient support macros with this AI prompt, covering repetitive tasks, trigger actions, customer messages, internal notes, and keyboard shortcuts.
